DISCOVERY 302, PLAINTIFF ADAM BRATT'S MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSE TO PLAINTIFFS FORM INTERROGATORIES, SET TWO AND FOR MONETARY SANCTIONS.PLAINTIFF/CROSS-DEFENDANT'S MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSES TO FORM INTERROGATORIES: MOTION GRANTED. NO OPPOSITION FILED. DEFENDANTS TO FILE FURTHER RESPONSES TO INTERROGATORIES15.1 AND 17.1 NEED TO BE RESPONDED TO AS TO EACH ITEM AT ISSUE AND AS TO EACH OF THE SUBPARTS (INCORPORATION BY REFERENCE IS ALLOWED) BUT PLAINTIFF/CROSS-DEFENDANT IS ENTITLED TO KNOW WHAT IS AT ISSUE WITH RESPECT TO EACH ITEM/CLAIM. RESPONSES TO BE MADE WITHIN 30 DAYS OF THE FILING OF THIS ORDER AND SANCTIONS AS SET FORTH BELOW.THE PREVAILING PARTY IS REQUIRED TO PREPARE A SINGLE ORDER FOR ALL 3 MOTIONS REPEATING VERBATIM THE SUBSTANTIVE PORTION OF THE TENTATIVE RULING AND MUST E-MAIL IT TO THE JUDGE PRO TEM AT RKANE1089@AOL.COM.JUDGE PRO TEM: ROBERT KANE, CLERK: M. WHEN THE COURT RESUMES NORMAL OPERATIONS, THE PRESIDING JUDGE WILL ANNOUNCE PROCEDURES FOR SETTING HEARING DATES FOR THOSE CASES WHICH HAVE BEEN FULLY BRIEFED. OPPOSITIONS WILL BE DUE BASED ON THE RESCHEDULED HEARING DATE, BUT NOTHING PRECLUDES A PARTY FROM FILING AN OPPOSITION EARLIER." (302)

LAW AND MOTION 302, PLAINTIFF ADAM BRATT'S MOTION TO COMPEL FUTHER RESPONSE TO REQUESTS FOR PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS, SET ONE AND FOR MONETARY SANCTIONS.THE COURT ADOPTS THE TENTATIVE RULING. PLAINTIFF ADAM BRATT'S MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSE TO REQUEST FOR PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS IS GRANTED. I LARGELY ADOPT THE JUDGE PRO TEM'S REPORT AND RECOMMENDATION.ALL OBJECTIONS ARE OVERRULED. DEFENDANT EBERE ANOSIKE SHALL PROVIDE A RESPONSE COMPLIANT WITH CCP 2031.210-230 AND PRODUCE DOCUMENTS NO LATER THAN SEPTEMBER 11, 2020. IF THE DOCUMENTS RESPONSIVE TO THE SUBJECT REQUEST HAVE ALREADY BEEN PRODUCED, DEFENDANT SHALL IDENTIFY THOSE DOCUMENTS BY PROVIDING "BATES"-STAMP NUMBERS AND PRODUCTION DATE(S). SANCTIONS ARE AWARDED IN THE AMOUNT OF $1,975 - TO BE PAID NO LATER THAN SEPTEMBER 11, 2020. COUNSEL FOR THE PLAINTIFF IS REQUIRED TO PREPARE A PROPOSED ORDER WHICH REPEATS VERBATIM THE SUBSTANTIVE PORTION OF THE COURT'S RULING APPROVED AS TO FORM.JUDGE: RICHARD B. ULMER JR., CLERK: M. GOODMAN, COURT REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO, CSR#8600, MARIA.TORREANO@GMAIL.COM, REPORTED. =(302/RBU)

Motion To Compel Futher Response To Requests For Production Of Documents, Set One And For Monetary Sanctions
Off Calendar Sep-04-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances (by Zoom):
David Henshaw, Esq. 408-533-1075 of Henshaw & Henry for Plaintiff.
Lisa Espada, Esq. 415-504-6195 for Defendant.

RULING - A R G U E D.(as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

PLAINTIFF ADAM BRATT's Motion To Compel Futher Response To Requests For Production Of Documents, Set One And For Monetary Sanctions.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.

Plaintiff Adam Bratt's motion to compel further response to request for production of documents is granted. I largely adopt the judge pro tem's report and recommendation.

All objections are overruled. Defendant Ebere Anosike shall provide a response compliant with CCP 2031.210-230 and produce documents no later than September 11, 2020. If the documents responsive to the subject request have already been produced, defendant shall identify those documents by providing "Bates"-stamp numbers and production date(s). Sanctions are awarded in the amount of $1,975 - to be paid no later than September 11, 2020.

Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling approved as to form.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/RBU)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Jul-15-2020 Continued To Sep-09-20 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jul-02-2020 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT EBERE ANOSIKE DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT.

No appearances made by the parties. The Court adopts its tentative ruling: Defendant Ebere Anosike's demurrer to plaintiff's second cause of action for fraud is overruled. Plaintiff pleads fraud with the requisite specificity.

The elements of fraud must be pled with particularity. (Beckwith v. Dahl (2012) 205 Cal.App.4th 1039; Lazar v. Superior Court (1996) 12 Cal.4th 631, 638 [the elements of fraud are (a) misrepresentation; (b) knowledge of falsity; (c) intent to defraud; (d) justifiable reliance; and (e) resulting damage].) In a promissory fraud action, to sufficiently allege the defendant made a misrepresentation, the complaint must allege that the defendant made a representation of intent to perform some future action and that the defendant did not really have that intent at the time the promise was made. (Beckwith v. Dahl, 205 Cal.App.4th at 1060.) Here, plaintiff alleges that beginning in August 2017, defendant began asking plaintiff for money to fund her business and for the use of plaintiff's credit cards to pay business expenses. (FAC 9.) These requests were made orally and in person. (Id.) The amended complaint details the dates of loans, the number of transactions, and the total amount loaned. (Id. 12, 15-18.) Plaintiff alleges defendant never intended to pay plaintiff back. (Id. 23.)

The speaker must intend not to honor the promise when the promise is made. (Beckwith, 205 Cal.App. 4th at 1062; Riversisland Cold Storage, Inc. v. Fresno-Madera Production Credit Assn. (2013) 55 Cal.4th 1169, 1183.) Mere failure to perform the promise later is not enough to show it was falsely made. (Tenzer v. Superscope, Inc. (1985) 39 Cal.3d 18, 30.) Evidence of fraudulent intent must be established directly, or more often circumstantially, with evidence of "defendant's insolvency, his hasty repudiation of the promise, his failure even to attempt performance, or his continued assurances after it was clear he would not perform." (Id.) Contrary to defendant's arguments, plaintiff does allege more than mere nonperformance of the promise. Plaintiff alleges defendant made regular promises that he would be paid back "very soon". (FAC 13 ["When plaintiff asked for repayment, defendant stated that she would pay plaintiff back when certain conditions would happen, including investment money she received for her business or sales form [sic] her business. However, these stories from defendant regularly changed."]; id. 23 ["With each loan, Defendant represented that she would repay the amounts she received from Plaintiff."]) Additionally, plaintiff alleges defendant never made any efforts to make any payment to plaintiff. (See id. 25 [in June 2019, defendant acknowledged in writing that she owed plaintiff the borrowed funds].)

Lastly, plaintiff alleges that because of his friendship with defendant, and defendant's regular promises and assurances, plaintiff believed defendant would repay the amounts owed. (FAC 13, 20.) The question of whether plaintiff's reliance was reasonable is a question of fact. (Blankenhelm v. E.F. Hutton & Co. (1990) 217 Cal.App.3d 1463, 1474.)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Anthony Vaughn, CSR# 6185, tonyvaughn@yahoo.com (302/EPS)
